title: Preventive Effects of Grape Polyphenols and Grapes Against Colon Cancer. Colorectal cancer is the second-leading cause of cancer death in men and women combined, accounting for 8 to 9 percent of all cancer mortality in the United States. Since there is a lack of effective treatment for advanced cancer, it is necessary to develop relatively safe and effective preventive agents that can inhibit or delay the progression of early-stage colorectal cancer. To this end, many grape polyphenols have been evaluated for their colorectal cancer-preventing potentials in preclinical cell and animal models. Many studies have documented the anticancer effects of purified bioactive polyphenols found in grapes, such as resveratrol and quercetin, against colon cancer in cell-based studies and animal models. Overall, these compounds have been shown to be able to block colon cancer cell proliferation, induce apoptosis, and suppress inflammation in colon cancer cells, as well as display chemopreventive actions in various animal models against colitis-associated colon cancer or sporadic colon cancer. In this article, we focus on the anticancer effects of grape extracts or whole grapes against colon cancer in the recent 5 years.

sentences.text: Preventive Effects of Grape Polyphenols and Grapes Against Colon Cancer.
Colorectal cancer is the second-leading cause of cancer death in men and women combined, accounting for 8 to 9 percent of all cancer mortality in the United States.

Since there is a lack of effective treatment for advanced cancer, it is necessary to develop relatively safe and effective preventive agents that can inhibit or delay the progression of early-stage colorectal cancer.
To this end, many grape polyphenols have been evaluated for their colorectal cancer-preventing potentials in preclinical cell and animal models.
Many studies have documented the anticancer effects of purified bioactive polyphenols found in grapes, such as resveratrol and quercetin, against colon cancer in cell-based studies and animal models.

Overall, these compounds have been shown to be able to block colon cancer cell proliferation, induce apoptosis, and suppress inflammation in colon cancer cells, as well as display chemopreventive actions in various animal models against colitis-associated colon cancer or sporadic colon cancer.
In this article, we focus on the anticancer effects of grape extracts or whole grapes against colon cancer in the recent 5 years.
Anticancer Effects of Whole Grapes, Grape Seed Extracts, and Anthocyanins.
In agreement with individual polyphenols, grape extracts or whole grapes have also been shown to display protective effects against colitis-associated colon cancer.

Researchers demonstrated that freeze-dried grape powder, prepared from a composite of fresh red, green, and black California grapes of seeded and seedless varieties, suppressed azoxymethane and dextran sulfate sodium-induced tumor multiplicity and colitis in mice when included at 10 percent in the diet, but not at 3 percent.

The grape powder supplement counteracted an azoxymethane and dextran sulfate sodium-induced decrease in bacterial evenness and dysbiosis and increased the relative abundance of butyrate-producing bacteria.

Moreover, supplementation with 0.1 percent anthocyanins was found to decrease the number of colon tumors induced by azoxymethane and dextran sulfate sodium and to significantly reduce the relative abundance of a pro-inflammatory microbe named Bilophila wadsworthia compared with the nonanthocyanin control in rats.
In another study, a grape seed extract was reported to reduce tumor burden and prevent gut barrier dysfunctions in azoxymethane and dextran sulfate sodium-induced colitis-associated colon cancer.

Grape pomace, which is a by-product of wine production and is rich in bioflavonoids and dietary fibers, has been shown to inhibit inflammation, suppress the proliferation of colon cancer cells, and block tumorigenesis in vivo.
For instance, it was reported that extracts prepared from grape pomace inhibited cell proliferation, enhanced cell cycle arrest, and induced apoptosis in colon cancer cells.
Furthermore, grape pomace water extract was found to decrease cell viability, lipopolysaccharide-induced pro-inflammatory genes and cytokines in colon cancer SW480 cells and isolated mouse colon.

Consistent with cell-based studies, researchers reported that dietary supplementation with 5 percent grape pomace in the diet suppressed azoxymethane and dextran sulfate sodium-induced tumor development and attenuated colonic inflammation in mice.
Resveratrol and Grape Polyphenols in Human Intervention Studies Against Colorectal Cancer.
Encouraged by promising preclinical data, clinical research has been conducted to evaluate the potential impact of resveratrol and grape powder on colon cancer.

In particular, in an open-label phase one pilot study, eight colon cancer patients were enrolled to examine the effects of a 14-day intervention with a plant-derived resveratrol formulation and resveratrol-containing freeze-dried grape powder on Wnt signaling in the colon.
The major findings show that resveratrol or freeze-dried grape powder significantly inhibited Wnt target gene expression in normal colonic mucosa.
The greatest effect on Wnt target gene expression was observed following the daily ingestion of 80 grams of grape powder.

In summary, grape polyphenols or grape powder has been shown to mitigate many intestinal disorders; exhibit antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and gut-barrier protecting actions; and modulate gut microbiota in preclinical cellular and animal models, as well as pilot human studies.
